items.foco: Definition: ascending infection of the female genital tract
items.title: Definition and route of infection
items.contentHtml:
Definition

  • Pelvic inflammatory disease is an ascending infection from the lower genital tract (vagina/cervix) to the endometrium, tubes, ovaries and pelvic peritoneum.
  • The main route is canalicular, favored by sexual intercourse, menstruation or instrumental procedures (curettage, insertion of an intrauterine device).
  • The answer Acute inflammatory disease can generate purulent exudate, edema and necrosis; Chronicity produces fibrosis and synechiae.
  • It is one of the most common preventable causes of tubal infertility in women of reproductive age.

items.foco: Most frequent agents: Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae
items.title: Main microbiology
items.contentHtml:
Etiological agents

  • Chlamydia trachomatis (serotypes D-K) and Neisseria gonorrhoeae are the primary pathogens in sexually transmitted pelvic inflammatory disease.
  • Infection with Chlamydia trachomatis is usually oligosymptomatic or asymptomatic, which delays diagnosis and increases the risk of injury tubal.
  • In up to 30-40% of cases, anaerobes (Bacteroides, Peptostreptococcus) and other aerobes (Gardnerella vaginalis, Escherichia coli) are isolated, especially in polymicrobial disease or associated with an intrauterine device.
  • Mycoplasma genitalium is also recognized as an emerging agent in non-pelvic inflammatory disease. gonococcal.

items.foco: Classic symptoms: bilateral pelvic pain and vaginal discharge
items.title: Typical clinical picture
items.contentHtml:
Classic symptoms

  • The cardinal symptom is bilateral pelvic pain of gradual, constant onset, which worsens with cervical mobilization and ambulation.
  • It is accompanied by mucopurulent or purulent vaginal discharge, sometimes with a bad odor if anaerobes are present.
  • There may be fever (temperature >38.3°C), intermenstrual bleeding, deep dyspareunia and dysuria.
  • It is key to remember that the absence of fever does not rule out pelvic inflammatory disease; Up to 40% of patients have a normal temperature.

items.foco: Positive Chandelier sign on bimanual examination
items.title: bimanual examination
items.contentHtml:
Chandelier's sign

  • The Chandelier's sign (or chandelier sign) is positive when the mobilization of the cervix during bimanual touch causes intense pain that the patient reports as a stabbing that radiates to both hypogastria.
  • It indicates pelvic peritoneal irritation and is highly suggestive of acute pelvic inflammatory disease.
  • It is associated with adnexal pain on palpation and a sensation of an adnexal mass if there is a tube-ovarian abscess.
  • The specificity of the sign is limited (it can appear in appendicitis, endometriosis or cyst rupture), but in the context of flow and fever it guides the diagnosis.

items.title: CDC Criteria
items.contentHtml:
Diagnostic criteria of the Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC)

  • Major criteria (minimum one for suspicion): pain on cervical mobilization, uterine pain or adnexal pain.
  • Additional criteria (increase specificity): temperature >38.3°C, mucopurulent cervical or vaginal discharge, leukocytosis in vaginal exudate (leukocytes >10 per 1000x field), elevation of C-reactive protein or erythrocyte sedimentation rate.
  • Definitive criteria: histopathological evidence of endometritis in biopsy, image (ultrasound or magnetic resonance imaging) showing tuboovarian abscess, or laparoscopic finding of tubal inflammation.
  • The diagnosis is fundamentally clinical; Microbiological confirmation is not required to initiate empirical treatment.

items.foco: Empiric outpatient treatment with ceftriaxone plus doxycycline
items.title: Outpatient regimen
items.contentHtml:
Outpatient empirical treatment

  • First-line regimen: ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscularly in a single dose (covers gonococcus) plus doxycycline 100 mg orally every 12 hours for 14 days (covers chlamydia).
  • If Mycoplasma genitalium infection is suspected, consider azithromycin 1 g orally in a single dose as an alternative to doxycycline, although resistance is increasing.
  • Treatment should be initiated upon clinical suspicion without waiting for microbiological results, to prevent sequelae.
  • Sexual abstinence is recommended until treatment is completed and clinical reassessment after 72 hours.

items.foco: Metronidazole added if anaerobes or tubeovarian abscess suspected
items.title: Anaerobic coverage
items.contentHtml:
Addition of metronidazole

  • Add metronidazole 500 mg orally every 12 hours for 14 days if anaerobes are suspected (foul discharge, tube-ovarian abscess, use of intrauterine device, severe pelvic inflammatory disease).
  • Anaerobic coverage is mandatory in tubovarian abscess or when polymicrobial infection is documented.
  • On an outpatient basis, metronidazole is combined with ceftriaxone and doxycycline (triple regimen).
  • Remember that metronidazole is bactericidal against strict anaerobes and should not be omitted if there are severity criteria.

items.title: Tuboovarian abscess
items.contentHtml:
Acute complication

  • The Tuboovarian abscess is a purulent collection that involves the tube and ovary, frequently polymicrobial (anaerobes + aerobes).
  • Clinically it manifests with intense pelvic pain, painful adnexal mass, high fever and leukocytosis.
  • The diagnosis is confirmed by transvaginal ultrasound (complex cystic image with internal echoes and wall thickening) or computed tomography.
  • Initial treatment is medical (triple regimen with metronidazole); If there is no response in 48-72 hours or if the abscess measures >4 cm, surgical or image-guided drainage is required.

items.foco: Chronic complication: infertility due to tubal obstruction
items.title: Tubal infertility
items.contentHtml:
Chronic complication

  • Infertility due to tubal obstruction is the most common sequelae of pelvic inflammatory disease, caused by fibrosis and intraluminal synechiae after inflammation.
  • The risk of infertility increases with each episode: one episode confers 8-12% risk; two episodes, 20-30%; three or more, >50%.
  • The obstruction usually affects the fimbrial or ampullary portion, and is diagnosed by hysterosalpingography (absent contrast extravasation) or laparoscopy.
  • Prevention through early diagnosis and treatment is the only effective measure to reduce this complication.

items.foco: Ectopic pregnancy as a late sequel
items.title: ectopic pregnancy
items.contentHtml:
Ectopic pregnancy

  • Pelvic inflammatory disease multiplies by 6-10 the risk of ectopic pregnancy due to tubal damage that alters the transport of the fertilized egg.
  • Partial obstruction or ciliary dysfunction favors implantation in the tube.
  • Clinically it presents with pelvic pain. unilateral, irregular vaginal bleeding and, in case of rupture, hypovolemic shock.
  • In any patient with a history of pelvic inflammatory disease and menstrual delay, ectopic pregnancy should be ruled out by quantitative human chorionic gonadotropin testing and transvaginal ultrasound.

items.foco: Hospitalization criteria: pregnancy, abscess, failure to oral treatment
items.title: Entry instructions
items.contentHtml:
Hospitalization criteria

  • Absolute indications for admission: pregnancy, tubovarian abscess, failure to oral treatment (persistence of fever or pain after 72 hours of outpatient antibiotics), or inability to tolerate oral administration.
  • Other criteria: severe illness with fever >39°C, nausea/vomiting that prevents ingestion, or diagnostic doubts that require laparoscopy.
  • In hospitalization, an intravenous regimen is used: ceftriaxone 1 g every 24 hours, plus doxycycline 100 mg every 12 hours, plus metronidazole 500 mg every 12 hours, until clinical improvement and then complete 14 days orally.
  • Clinical reassessment after 48 hours is mandatory to decide whether to continue intravenous management or switch to oral treatment.

items.foco: Sexually transmitted disease: study and treatment of the couple
items.title: Couple management
items.contentHtml:
Study and treatment of the couple

  • Pelvic inflammatory disease is a sexually transmitted infection; Therefore, the sexual partner(s) of the last 60 days should be evaluated and treated empirically against Chlamydia trachomatis and Neisseria gonorrhoeae.
  • Scheme for the couple: ceftriaxone 500 mg intramuscular single dose plus doxycycline 100 mg every 12 hours for 7 days (or azithromycin 1 g oral if chlamydia coverage is preferred).
  • Sexual abstinence should be recommended until the patient and partner have completed treatment (7 days after the last dose if doxycycline was used).
  • Reporting to health authorities is mandatory in many countries; Prevention counseling and testing for human immunodeficiency virus and syphilis should be offered.
